title: Pentatonic Minor — Shape 1
description: The minor pentatonic scale in its first position is the most-played scale in rock guitar. Five notes, five frets, two notes per string in a compact box shape. This is the starting point of virtually every lead guitar journey.
practice: Learn Shape 1 of the minor pentatonic in A (frets 5-8 on all strings).
Play up and down the scale cleanly at 60 BPM with a metronome.
Improvise over a one-chord Am vamp using only Shape 1. Focus on making it musical, not running scales.

title: Pentatonic Major — Shape 1
description: The major pentatonic has a brighter, country-and-folk character. Shape 1 is the minor pentatonic shape shifted three frets down — the same five strings, same two-per-string pattern, but a different root and a completely different emotional quality.
practice: Play A major pentatonic (Shape 1 starting at the 2nd fret). Compare it to A minor pentatonic.
Improvise over a D major chord using major pentatonic — notice how the 2nd scale degree adds sweetness.
Switch between major and minor pentatonic in the same solo, targeting chord tones.

title: The Blues Scale
description: The blues scale adds one note to the minor pentatonic — the flat 5th, called the 'blue note.' This single addition creates the signature tension and grit of blues and rock guitar. The note is used as a passing tone, not a resting point.
practice: Add the flat 5th (flat 5 = b5) to your A minor pentatonic shape. Feel where it sits.
Play the scale using the blue note as a brief passing tone — not a target note.
Improvise over a 12-bar blues using the blues scale. Use the flat 5 sparingly for effect.

title: Phrasing — Using Space
description: A phrase is a musical sentence — a group of notes that begins, develops, and ends. Beginners fill every beat; good phrasing uses rests deliberately. Space creates anticipation and makes each note more meaningful.
practice: Play a 4-beat phrase, then rest for 4 beats. Repeat. Make the rests feel intentional.
Listen to a Stevie Ray Vaughan solo and count the rests. Notice how long they last.
Record a 30-second improvisation. Review: are you playing continuously, or is there space?

title: Call and Response
description: Call and response is a two-phrase structure where the first phrase 'asks a question' (ending on tension) and the second 'answers' (ending on resolution). It's the foundation of blues conversation and appears in virtually every musical tradition.
practice: Play a 2-bar phrase ending on the 2nd degree (tension). Answer it with a phrase ending on the root.
Improvise in a strict A-B-A-B pattern: 2 bars phrase, 2 bars response.
Record your call-and-response improvisation and evaluate: does the answer feel satisfying?

title: Target Notes
description: A target note is a chord tone played on a strong beat that your phrase is aiming toward. Improvising toward target notes rather than 'playing the scale' is what makes a solo feel connected to the harmony underneath.
practice: Over a Dm chord, play phrases that land on D, F, or A on beats 1 or 3.
Over a G7 chord, target G, B, D, or F as landing notes.
Play a ii-V-I (Dm-G7-C) and target a different chord tone on each chord change.

title: Motif Development
description: A motif is a short melodic idea (2–6 notes) that can be repeated, transposed, inverted, or rhythmically varied to build a coherent solo. This is the difference between playing licks and playing music.
practice: Create a 3-note motif. Play it, then play it one step higher. Then one step lower. Keep the rhythm identical.
Take a motif and double its rhythmic values — turn eighth notes into quarter notes.
Improvise an entire 12-bar solo using only one motif, developed in different ways.
